name: barbell squat jump step rear lunge
instructions: Start by standing with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell across your upper back.
Lower your body into a squat position by bending your knees and pushing your hips back.
Explode upwards, jumping off the ground as high as you can.
Land softly on your feet and immediately step back with one leg into a reverse lunge.
Lower your body down until your front thigh is parallel to the ground, keeping your back straight.
Push through your front heel to return to the starting position.
Repeat the jump and lunge sequence on the other leg.
Continue alternating legs for the desired number of repetitions.

Explode upwards, jumping off the ground as high as you can. Land softly on your feet and immediately step back with one leg into a reverse lunge. Lower your body down until your front thigh is parallel to the ground, keeping your back straight. Push through your front heel to return to the starting position. Repeat the jump and lunge sequence on the other leg. Continue alternating legs for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Axial loading under fatigue may worsen asymmetrical curves. Prefer split-stance or supported variations.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Loaded spinal flexion or heavy axial loading should be avoided. Use supported variations.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
